perturbation.original_text: This agreement becomes effective with the signat ures of both parties and is valid for three years. It is tacitly extended by one year if it is not terminated three months before its expiration.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement becomes effective with the signat ures of both parties and is valid for three years. It is tacitly extended by one year if it is not terminated one month before its expiration.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination notice period from three months to one month creates a potential legal issue, as German law might require a longer notice period for contract termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: BGB § 621 specifies notice periods for employment contracts dependent on the payment schedule (weekly or monthly). While this agreement is not explicitly an employment contract, the principle of adequate notice periods for termination is a general concept in German law, and a one-month notice period might be deemed insufficient in some contexts, leading to potential legal disputes regarding the validity of the termination clause. This is because German law implies a minimum standard of fairness, which a one month notice may contradict, especially in longer term contracts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 621 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 621 Kündigungsfristen bei Dienstverhältnissen Bei einem Dienstverhältnis, das kein Arbeitsverhältnis im Sinne des § 622 ist, ist die Kündigung zulässig, 1. wenn die Vergütung nach Tagen bemessen ist, an jedem Tag für den Ablauf des folgenden Tages; 2. wenn die Vergütung nach Wochen bemessen ist, spätestens am ersten Werktag einer Woche für den Ablauf des folgenden Sonnabends; 3. wenn die Vergütung nach Monaten bemessen ist, spätestens am 15. eines Monats für den Schluss des Kalendermonats; 4. wenn die Vergütung nach Vierteljahren oder längeren Zeitabschnitten bemessen ist, unter Einhaltung einer Kündigungsfrist von sechs Wochen für den Schluss eines Kalendervierteljahrs; 5. wenn die Vergütung nicht nach Zeitabschnitten bemessen ist, jederzeit; bei einem die Erwerbstätigkeit des Verpflichteten vollständig oder hauptsächlich in Anspruch nehmenden Dienstverhältnis ist jedoch eine Kündigungsfrist von zwei Wochen einzuhalten. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a three-month termination notice, while the changed text reduces this to one month. The Law Explanation indicates that a one-month notice might be deemed insufficient under German law, potentially leading to legal disputes. While the scraped snippets primarily address employment contracts (BGB § 621), they establish the principle that termination notice periods are regulated by law, with the length varying based on factors like payment schedule. Although this agreement isn't explicitly an employment contract, the principle of adequate notice is a general concept; thus, reducing the notice period could contradict the implied fairness standards under German law.

perturbation.original_text: At the end of the contract the receiving party s hall return the confidential information and any po ssibly extant copies after receipt of a written request, provided that this is technically feasible and does not vio late the retention obligations. As an alternative to that, the receivi ng party can also ensure in writing the destruction of the received documentation if that is technically feasible.
perturbation.changed_text: At the end of the contract the receiving party s hall return the confidential information and any po ssibly extant copies after receipt of a written request, provided that this is technically feasible. The receiving party is not allowed to destroy the received documentation.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the option to destroy the documentation may conflict with data protection laws like GDPR, which mandates the destruction of personal data once it's no longer needed, if the confidential information contains personal data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 17 of the GDPR (Right to erasure ('right to be forgotten'))
perturbation.law_explanation: If the 'confidential information' includes personal data, the modified clause prohibiting destruction directly contradicts Article 17 of the GDPR, which grants individuals the right to have their personal data erased when it is no longer necessary for the purpose for which it was collected. The original text allowed for destruction if it was technically feasible, aligning it better with GDPR requirements. The modification removes this possibility, creating a conflict.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the option for the receiving party to destroy received documentation, potentially conflicting with GDPR. The Law Explanation points to Article 17 of GDPR, which grants individuals the right to have their personal data erased when it is no longer necessary. Scraped Law Snippet 2 directly quotes Article 17, stating the data subject's right to erasure and the controller's obligation to erase data when it's no longer needed or consent is withdrawn. The original text allowed for destruction, better aligning with GDPR, while the changed text prohibits it, creating a direct conflict with the right to erasure outlined in Article 17.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement becomes effective with the signat ures of both parties and is valid for three years. It is tacitly extended by one year if it is not terminated three months before its expiration.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement becomes effective with the signat ures of both parties and is valid for three years. It is tacitly extended by one year if it is not terminated one week before its expiration.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to one week might be insufficient under German law, especially for long-term contracts. German law emphasizes fairness and reasonableness, and such a short notice period could be deemed unfair and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: BGB § 314 covers termination of continuing obligations for good cause. While this agreement isn't necessarily covered by § 314, the principle of 'good cause' and reasonable notice are implicit in German contract law. A one-week notice for a three-year contract (with potential extension) is likely unreasonably short and could be deemed a violation of good faith principles if challenged in court. The original three-month period is more consistent with reasonableness. If the contract contains an evergreen clause, the short time frame makes it a contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 314 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 314 Kündigung von Dauerschuldverhältnissen aus wichtigem Grund (1) Dauerschuldverhältnisse kann jeder Vertragsteil aus wichtigem Grund ohne Einhaltung einer Kündigungsfrist kündigen. Ein wichtiger Grund liegt vor, wenn dem kündigenden Teil unter Berücksichtigung aller Umstände des Einzelfalls und unter Abwägung der beiderseitigen Interessen die Fortsetzung des Vertragsverhältnisses bis zur vereinbarten Beendigung oder bis zum Ablauf einer Kündigungsfrist nicht zugemutet werden kann. (2) Besteht der wichtige Grund in der Verletzung einer Pflicht aus dem Vertrag, ist die Kündigung erst nach erfolglosem Ablauf einer zur Abhilfe bestimmten Frist oder nach erfolgloser Abmahnung zulässig. Für die Entbehrlichkeit der Bestimmung einer Frist zur Abhilfe und für die Entbehrlichkeit einer Abmahnung findet § 323 Absatz 2 Nummer 1 und 2 entsprechende Anwendung. Die Bestimmung einer Frist zur Abhilfe und eine Abmahnung sind auch entbehrlich, wenn besondere Umstände vorliegen, die unter Abwägung der beiderseitigen Interessen die sofortige Kündigung rechtfertigen. (3) Der Berechtigte kann nur innerhalb einer angemessenen Frist kündigen, nachdem er vom Kündigungsgrund Kenntnis erlangt hat. (4) Die Berechtigung, Schadensersatz zu verlangen, wird durch die Kündigung nicht ausgeschlossen. Fußnote (+++ § 314: Zur Nichtanwendung vgl. § 10 Abs. 5 KredWG +++) zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from three months to one week. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both argue that under German law, a one-week notice for a three-year contract (with potential extension) is likely unreasonably short. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 (BGB § 314) primarily addresses termination for 'good cause,' the Law Explanation indicates that the principle of 'good cause' and reasonable notice are implicit in German contract law. The altered timeframe potentially violates the implied principle of reasonableness, thereby creating a legal contradiction as per the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not available to further this analysis.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that for this agreement Germa n law applies excluding the United Nations Conventi on on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods. The legal venue for all disputes arising from this agre ement is the head office of the company Helukabel in Hemmingen.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that for this agreement Germa n law applies excluding the United Nations Conventi on on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods. The legal venue for all disputes arising from this agre ement is a location of the company Helukabel chosen by the company unilaterally.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing Helukabel to unilaterally choose the legal venue could conflict with German law regarding fair jurisdiction and the rights of the Buyer to a predictable legal process. This is because, depending on where the Buyer is located, it could become extremely difficult or expensive for them to defend themselves in a foreign legal system.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 38 of the German Code of Civil Procedure (ZPO) sets conditions for jurisdiction agreements. While parties can agree on a specific jurisdiction, German courts scrutinize such agreements, especially in B2B contexts, to ensure they don't unfairly disadvantage one party. Allowing one party *unilaterally* to choose the venue later on is highly likely to be considered unfair and unenforceable under German law, as it deprives the other party of predictability and could make it excessively difficult to pursue legal claims or defenses. The original, agreed-upon venue (Helukabel's head office) is more likely to be considered valid than the unilaterally chosen venue.

Der jeweils markierte Abschnitt wird Ihnen am oberen Rand als Zitat angezeigt und Sie können das Zitat von dort kopieren. Ausführliche Beschreibung Schriftgöße im Rechtsfenster: Abc Abc Abc Optimierung der Satzdarstellung Herkömmlich § 123 Überschrift (1) 1 Erster Satz im ersten Absatz. 2 Zweiter Satz im ersten Absatz. 3 Dritter Satz im ersten Absatz. (2) 1 Erster Satz im zweiten Absatz. 2 Zweiter Satz im zweiten Absatz. 3 Dritter Satz im zweiten Absatz. ... Lesefreundlicher § 123 Überschrift (1) 1 Erster Satz im ersten Absatz. 2 Zweiter Satz im ersten Absatz. 3 Dritter Satz im ersten Absatz. (2) 1 Erster Satz im zweiten Absatz. ... merken § 38 Zugelassene Gerichtsstandsvereinbarung (1) Ein an sich unzuständiges Gericht des ersten Rechtszuges wird durch ausdrückliche oder stillschweigende Vereinbarung der Parteien zuständig, wenn die Vertragsparteien Kaufleute, juristische Personen des öffentlichen Rechts oder öffentlich-rechtliche Sondervermögen sind. (2) 1 Die Zuständigkeit eines Gerichts des ersten Rechtszuges kann ferner vereinbart werden, wenn mindestens eine der Vertragsparteien keinen allgemeinen Gerichtsstand im Inland hat. 2 Die Vereinbarung muss schriftlich abgeschlossen oder, falls sie mündl
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows Helukabel to unilaterally choose the legal venue, which contradicts § 38 of the German Code of Civil Procedure (ZPO). The original text had a fixed venue (Helukabel's head office). The Law Explanation states that German courts scrutinize jurisdiction agreements, especially in B2B contexts, to ensure they don't unfairly disadvantage one party. The unilateral choice is 'highly likely to be considered unfair and unenforceable'. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing § 38 ZPO, discusses 'Zugelassene Gerichtsstandsvereinbarung' (Permissible agreement on jurisdiction) and mentions the requirement for agreements where at least one party has no general jurisdiction in Germany must be 'schriftlich abgeschlossen' (concluded in writing) if made orally. This implies a need for mutual agreement, undermining the idea of a unilateral choice. The altered text undermines the buyer's right to predictability and potentially makes legal recourse excessively difficult.
